title: Amy Allen, the Hitmaker Behind Sabrina Carpenter and ‘APT.,’ Is ASCAP’s Songwriter of the Year
summary: If you’ve had “APT.” stuck in your head for months, you have Amy Allen to thank—and now she has a shiny new trophy to show for it. At the ASCAP Pop Music Awards in Hollywood on Thursday, Allen was crowned Songwriter of the Year, a recognition of her role in crafting some of the year’s most ubiquitous hits. Her credits include Sabrina Carpenter’s “Manchild,” “Tears,” and “Bed Chem,” as well as the Rosé and Bruno Mars collaboration “APT.,” a song so infectious it’s practically a global earworm. Presenting the award, legendary producer Jimmy Jam drew a line between human artistry and artificial intelligence. “We don’t have to worry about AI because of people like Amy Allen,” he said, underscoring the irreplaceable spark of real songwriting. The evening also honored Laufey (pronounced lay-vay), the Icelandic-Chinese singer who has carved a unique niche by fusing pop with classical and jazz influences. She received the ASCAP Creative Voice Award, presented by Suki Waterhouse. “I’m looking around and I’m seeing the word ‘pop,’” Laufey said from the stage. “I loved pop music growing up, and I always knew that there was a way for me to find my space in pop music. I grew up loving classical, loving jazz, knowing that was the pop music of its time. And I had this dream to make it the pop music of now, of this decade, and I really feel that has happened.” Sony Music Publishing was named Pop Music Publisher of the Year, with hits from Alex Warren, Olivia Dean, Kehlani, and Chappell Roan. Other winners included Jack Antonoff (for work with Taylor Swift and Kendrick Lamar), Max Martin, Justin Bieber, and Timbaland—a reminder that behind every earworm is a writer who dreamed it up first.
why_it_matters: This awards ceremony shines a light on the often invisible people who create the songs that dominate our playlists. In an era when artificial intelligence can churn out melodies in seconds, Amy Allen’s success is a powerful reminder that human creativity, emotion, and personal story are still at the heart of the music that truly moves us—and that might influence what kind of music industries we support as listeners.

keywords.explanation: A catchy piece of music that keeps repeating in a person’s mind.
Impossible to replace because something is unique or too valuable.
A specialized segment of the market or a particular area of interest.
Blending together different styles or elements to create something new.
Creative skill and imagination in a field like music or painting.

questions.question: Which award did Amy Allen receive at the ASCAP Pop Music Awards?
What is the title of the collaboration between Rosé and Bruno Mars that Amy Allen co-wrote?
Who presented the Songwriter of the Year award to Amy Allen?
What musical genres does Laufey fuse in her music?
Which company was named Pop Music Publisher of the Year?
What did Jimmy Jam say about Amy Allen during the ceremony?
options: That we don’t have to worry about AI because of people like her.
That she is the most streamed artist of the decade.
That she should perform her own songs.
questions.correct_answer: That we don’t have to worry about AI because of people like her.

background_read: ASCAP, the American Society of Composers, Authors and Publishers, was founded in 1914 and is one of the oldest performing rights organizations, collecting royalties for songwriters when their music is played publicly.

Sabrina Carpenter (an American singer and actress who started on Disney Channel and later became a pop star), Rosé (a member of the Korean girl group Blackpink who launched a solo career in 2021), and Bruno Mars (a Grammy-winning American singer-songwriter known for hits like 'Uptown Funk') are among the artists who performed Allen’s songs.

Jimmy Jam (half of the legendary production duo Jimmy Jam and Terry Lewis, who crafted many of Janet Jackson’s biggest hits) presented the award and drew attention to the growing debate about artificial intelligence in music creation, insisting that human songwriters possess an irreplaceable spark.

Laufey (an Icelandic-Chinese singer, born in Reykjavik, educated at Berklee College of Music) has built a following by blending pop melodies with classical orchestration and jazz harmonies; her recognition reflects a broader trend of genre-fluid artists rewriting the boundaries of pop music.
Article_Structure: LEAD: Amy Allen, the writer behind 'APT.' and multiple Sabrina Carpenter tracks, is named ASCAP Songwriter of the Year.
└─ specific: The song is described as 'practically a global earworm.'

├─ QUOTE: "We don’t have to worry about AI because of people like Amy Allen."
└─ SIGNIFICANCE: Positions her work as irreplaceable in an era of machine-made music.
SHIFT: Laufey receives the ASCAP Creative Voice Award.
├─ WHO: Icelandic-Chinese singer who fuses classical/jazz with pop.
├─ QUOTE: She dreamed of making classical and jazz "the pop music of now."
└─ PRESENTER: Suki Waterhouse (English actress and singer).
INDUSTRY RECOGNITION: Sony Music Publishing wins publisher of the year.
└─ DETAIL: Cited for hits by Alex Warren, Olivia Dean, Kehlani, Chappell Roan.
WIDER CONTEXT: Other winners include Jack Antonoff, Max Martin, Justin Bieber, Timbaland.
CLOSING TAKEAWAY: Behind every earworm is a writer who dreamed it up first.

perspectives.description: Honoring a hitmaker like Allen finally gives behind-the-scenes songwriters the credit they deserve. It validates that writing for other artists is a legitimate and celebrated career path, and that the emotional punch of a hit often comes from the pen of a person who may never appear in the music video.

Despite these awards, the music industry still systematically underpays songwriters through low streaming royalty rates and opaque licensing deals. Many creators earn far less than performers, and a single trophy does little to fix the structural imbalance that makes it hard for most songwriters to make a stable living.

Laufey’s genre-fusing success and Allen’s cross-artist songwriting highlight a broader shift: today’s pop market rewards eclecticism and cultural blending. The rise of TikTok and streaming has broken down the old genre silos, so artists who draw from classical, jazz, K-pop, and indie can all coexist under the same 'pop' umbrella—a transformation that is reshaping how records are made and marketed.

As AI songwriting tools become more sophisticated, the unique value of human songwriters like Allen will be tested. The next decade may see a hybrid model where AI handles routine production tasks while humans focus on deeply personal storytelling, but only if copyright laws and royalty systems evolve to protect human creators from being replaced or undervalued.
